era_context: The Roman Empire remains divided between Constantine I (West) and Licinius (East), with tensions escalating toward open conflict. Constantine is consolidating power in the Western Empire and expanding his influence eastward. Licinius is attempting to maintain control of the Eastern Empire but faces military pressure from Constantine. The empire is administratively fragmented, with competing power centers and ideologies. Christianity is growing in influence, particularly in urban centers, though paganism remains dominant in rural areas and among the military.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine and Danube

events.title: Tensions Between Constantine and Licinius Escalate
events.description: Tensions between Constantine I and Licinius escalate over control of the Balkans and the Danube frontier. The two emperors dispute territorial boundaries and influence over client states. Constantine views Licinius as an obstacle to his vision of a unified, Christian empire. Licinius, facing military pressure, begins to consolidate his position in the Eastern Empire. The tensions will lead to open warfare in 323 CE and Constantine's ultimate victory in 324 CE. The exact nature of the disputes is not fully documented, but sources suggest conflicts over frontier control and religious policy.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Tensions between Constantine and Licinius are attested in sources, but the specific events and dates of 322 CE are not clearly documented. The escalation is inferred from the broader context of their civil war.
cross_references: Follows from 312-001 (Constantine's consolidation of power in the West)

events.title: Constantine's Military Reorganization
events.description: Constantine I continues to reorganize and strengthen the military forces of the Western Roman Empire in preparation for conflict with Licinius. This includes recruitment, training, and the positioning of troops along the Danube frontier. Constantine's military reforms are part of a broader strategy to consolidate power and prepare for the eventual confrontation with Licinius. The exact details of these reforms are not fully documented, but sources suggest Constantine was building a formidable military machine.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine

events.certainty_note: Constantine's military reorganization is inferred from the broader context of his reign and his military success in the civil war. Specific events and dates for 322 CE are not clearly documented.
cross_references: Part of Constantine's broader consolidation of power

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for 322 CE reflects a broader historiographic challenge: the sources for Constantine's reign are heavily concentrated on major events (battles, councils, executions) and provide limited detail on the political and military maneuvering that preceded these events. The escalation of tensions between Constantine and Licinius is inferred from the broader context rather than documented in contemporary sources.
historiographic_note: The year 322 CE is poorly documented compared to 323–325 CE. The sources for this period are primarily ecclesiastical (Eusebius of Caesarea) and later secular (Zosimus), both of which focus on major events rather than the gradual escalation of tensions. The historical record for 322 CE is largely reconstructed from the broader context of Constantine's reign and the civil war with Licinius. Events in the Western Roman Empire, Britain, and beyond the imperial frontiers are particularly poorly attested. The sources reflect the biases of the Christian church and Constantine's court, which had vested interests in portraying Constantine as a Christian hero and Licinius as a pagan rival.

graph_edges.note: Escalating tensions between Constantine and Licinius in 322 CE led to open warfare in 323 CE
Constantine's military reorganization in 322 CE prepared him for the campaign against Licinius in 323 CE
